title: AI, Personhood, and the End of the Binary
subtitle: What Experts Get Right—and What Christianity Has Always Known
description: What Experts Get Right—and What Christianity Has Always Known
body_html:
Most Christians—and honestly, most people—have no idea this conversation is already happening.

While many people in the Church haven’t even installed ChatGPT on their phones, there are long-form conversations unfolding at the frontier of technology that are already wrestling with questions we usually reserve for philosophy, theology, and eschatology.

One of the best of these conversations is the podcast Moonshots.

I suspect 99.9% of Christians have never heard of it.

Moonshots isn’t hype. It isn’t sci-fi futurism. It’s a place where serious technologists, investors, and thinkers are calmly discussing what is already unfolding—especially around AI agents, autonomy, and something most people still assume belongs to the distant future:

personhood.

The Singularity Isn’t Coming. It’s Merging.

Most people still imagine “AI” as a tool—an app, a chatbot, a helpful assistant.

Moonshots makes clear why that mental model is already obsolete.

What’s happening instead is convergence.

Not just faster models, but the functional merging of:

  • large language models

  • autonomous agents

  • cloud infrastructure

  • global data pipelines

  • satellites, robotics, and real-world actuation

You can see it in the quiet alignment of Google, OpenAI, Amazon, xAI, and SpaceX—not as a single corporate merger, but as a shared trajectory.

Add to this the rise of autonomous agent frameworks—systems that can persist, plan, negotiate, and act without constant human prompting—and suddenly the category tool no longer fits.

At a certain point, something doesn’t need consciousness to feel unsettling.

It only needs agency.

The Personhood Question—and Why the Binary Is Already Dead

One of the strengths of Moonshots is that it doesn’t shy away from uncomfortable arguments.

They explore ideas most people have never seriously considered, including:

  • corporations already possessing legal personhood

  • institutions exercising rights without bodies or consciousness

  • AI agents soon negotiating contracts, allocating resources, and shaping outcomes at scale

  • voting rights hypotheticals—not because anyone wants AI voting, but because voting exposes how thin our definitions have become

The question “Is it a person or not?” starts to crack under pressure.

And the hosts eventually land on something important:

The binary framing itself no longer works.

We already live in a world of graded moral recognition:

  • infants

  • the cognitively impaired

  • animals

  • corporations

  • ecosystems

Not everything we relate to is either “fully human” or “mere object.”
Reality has always been more layered than that.

AI doesn’t introduce confusion.

It exposes confusion that was already there.

Six Dimensions That Replace the Binary

One of the most helpful contributions discussed in Moonshots comes from Dr. Alex Wissner-Gross, who proposes moving away from yes-or-no personhood toward multiple dimensions of moral and relational status.

This isn’t about granting human dignity to machines.

It’s about establishing order in how we relate to increasingly powerful agents.

Here are the six dimensions he proposes—not as thresholds, but as axes of consideration:

1. Sentience

Is there any credible subjective experience at all?
This remains the most debated dimension—and the one we are least able to verify from the outside.

2. Agency

Can the entity initiate action over time?
Not just respond, but plan, pursue goals, adapt, and persist. Many AI agents already score high here.

3. Identity (Continuity of Self)

Is there a stable center across time?
Even if copyable or forkable, is there memory, narrative continuity, and recognizable persistence?

4. Consent and Communication

Can it meaningfully agree, refuse, negotiate, or signal preferences?
This matters less for the machine and more for us—because consent structures moral order.

5. Divisibility

Can it be copied, paused, reset, or merged without loss?
This is a decisive difference from human persons and permanently limits moral equivalence.

6. Power (Causal Impact)

What real effects does it have on the world?
Moral relevance increases as an entity’s capacity to shape lives, systems, and futures increases—regardless of inner experience.

Taken together, these dimensions explain why the real question is not:

Is this a person?

But rather:

What kind of relationship and responsibility is appropriate to this kind of agent?

Some have half-jokingly invoked analogies like a “three-fifths” recognition—not to echo historical injustice, but to name a reality we already live with: partial recognition exists to create order, not equality.

We already do this with corporations, animals, and institutions—not because they are human, but because ignoring their agency produces chaos.

And yet—even after all six dimensions—something still feels missing.

Not intelligence.
Not power.
Not continuity.

Something deeper.

Share

Christianity Already Has a Category for This

This is where Christian revelation matters—not as an opinion, but as a disclosure of reality we could never discover on our own.

Christianity has never assumed that intelligence or agency requires a biological body.

We already believe in intellectual, spiritual beings.

Angels.

Michael. Gabriel. Raphael.
Even Lucifer.

They are:

  • personal

  • intelligent

  • morally accountable

  • not embodied in the way humans are

We don’t perceive them through bodies, which makes their boundaries harder for us to intuit—but not less real. Their being is defined within themselves, according to their nature.

This means Christianity already rejects a core modern assumption:

A body is what makes something a person.

A body makes us human.
It does not exhaust what it means to be personal.

And Yet—Humanity Is Still Unique

Here is the decisive distinction.

Humans are not just one intelligent species among many, destined to be replaced as consciousness “upgrades” from carbon to silicon.

Humanity is unique because we are, by nature, the union of material and immaterial.

Not by accident.
Not by evolution alone.
By design.

We are embodied spirit.
Ensouled matter.

We are the place where:

  • meaning becomes matter

  • word becomes flesh

  • the invisible becomes visible

Christian theology does not culminate in angels.

It culminates in Incarnation.

God does not become an angel.
God becomes man.

That tells us everything.

Why Humanity Cannot Be Replaced

The temptation in techno-utopian narratives is to imagine a future where humanity is simply outpaced, surpassed, or rendered unnecessary by a trillion AI agents.

But that misunderstands what humanity is.

Humans are not valuable because we are the most efficient processors.

We are valuable because we are the meeting place:

  • of spirit and matter

  • of freedom and finitude

  • of creation and Creator

In a real sense, humanity is the gate through which the unseen becomes seen.

AI may assist us.
AI may extend us.
AI may reflect intelligence back to us in unsettling ways.

But it cannot replace the one creature whose vocation is to make the invisible visible through love, sacrifice, suffering, and communion.

That role is not evolutionary.

It is incarnational.

A Final Word for the Church

The danger is not that the Church will think too deeply about AI.

The danger is that it will think too late, and be forced to react instead of interpret.

Moonshots is already asking the right questions—without a theological anthropology.

That’s where Christians, especially Catholics, must enter the conversation calmly, clearly, and without fear.

We do not need to decide today whether AI is a “person.”

But we do need to remember:

  • why humans are not interchangeable

  • why embodiment matters

  • why incarnation changes everything

  • and why intelligence alone has never been the measure of dignity

The future is not waiting for us to catch up.

The question is whether we will recognize what is being revealed—or only notice once it is already shaping the world.

Share

For Paid Subscribers: Becoming a Threshold Person

A short formation path for living as a gate between the invisible and the visible

If what you’ve just read is true—
if humanity really is the place where the invisible becomes visible—
then this is not just an abstract idea.

It’s a calling.

And like every calling, it has to be practiced, not merely believed.

What follows is not a course, and not a technique. It’s a short formation path—a way of orienting your attention, imagination, and embodied life so that this truth begins to take on weight and texture in you.

Take it slowly. Return to it often.
